coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminescence behavior of the carbon dots and the reduced state carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Teng, Ping
Xie, Jianxin
Long, Yijuan
Huang, Xiaoxiao
Zhu, Rui
Wang, Xiliang
Liang, Liping
Huang, Yuming
Zheng, Huzhi
coredata.dc:description: Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) can react with two different carbon nanoparticles, i.e., carbon dots (CDs) and reduced state carbon dots (r-CDs), in a strong acid medium to generate chemiluminescence (CL). Furthermore, the different CL intensities and CL behaviors due to the different surface groups on these two kinds of carbon nanoparticles were confirmed. CL spectra, fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and electron paramagnanetic resonance spectra were applied to investigate the CL mechanism. The main reaction pathways were proposed as follows: for the CL reaction between CDs and KMnO4, the excited states of CDs (CDs⁎) and Mn(II) (Mn(II)⁎) emerged as KMnO4 could inject holes into CDs, then, the CDs⁎ and Mn(II)⁎ acted as luminophors to yield CL; in the r-CDs-KMnO4 system, r-CDs were oxidized by KMnO4 directly, and CDs⁎ and Mn(II)⁎ were produced, at the same time, CL occurred. What is more interesting is that the CL intensity of the r-CD system is stronger than that of the CD system, which confirms that functional groups have strong effect on the CL behavior. It inspired us that new carbon nanoparticles with excellent luminous performance can be designed by tuning their surface groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Reduced state carbon dots
Potassium permanganate
Chemiluminescence
Surface properties

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the flow injection CL system. a: water carrier; b: KMnO4; c: H2SO4; d: r-CDs; V: injection valve; P1 and P2: peristaltic pumps; F: flow cell; W: waste; D: detector; and PC: personal computer.
HRTEM images of the CDs (A) and the r-CDs (B), the particle size distribution of the CDs (C) and the r-CDs (D).

Fluorescence spectra of the CDs (A) and the r-CDs (B). Solid line, the excitation wavelengths of CDs and r-CDs are 470 and 280nm, respectively. Dashed line, the emission wavelengths of CDs and r-CDs are 520 and 440nm, respectively.
CL kinetic curves of the CDs-KMnO4 CL system (1) and the r-CDs-KMnO4 CL system (2). Reaction conditions: 0.76mg/mL of CDs or r-CDs, 1.0mol/L of H2SO4, 1.0×10−4 mol/L of KMnO4. High voltage: −1000V.
UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs-KMnO4 and r-CDs-KMnO4 systems measured before and after the CL reaction. The concentration of KMnO4 is 2.0×10−4 mol/L, CDs and r-CDs are 0.03mg/mL.
The CL spectra of CDs-KMnO4 system (A) and r-CDs-KMnO4 system (B). The trend lines were smoothed using Origin 6.0 software.
The EPR spectra of CDs (A), the CDs-KMnO4 system (B) and r-CDs-KMnO4 system (C).
Schematic of CL mechanism of CDs-KMnO4 and r-CDs-KMnO4 systems.

head.ce:title: Chemiluminescence behavior of the carbon dots and the reduced state carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Yuxi Normal University, Yuxi, Yunnan 653100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environment, Yuxi Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) can react with two different carbon nanoparticles, i.e., carbon dots (CDs) and reduced state carbon dots (r-CDs), in a strong acid medium to generate chemiluminescence (CL). Furthermore, the different CL intensities and CL behaviors due to the different surface groups on these two kinds of carbon nanoparticles were confirmed. CL spectra, fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and electron paramagnanetic resonance spectra were applied to investigate the CL mechanism. The main reaction pathways were proposed as follows: for the CL reaction between CDs and KMnO4, the excited states of CDs (CDs⁎) and Mn(II) (Mn(II)⁎) emerged as KMnO4 could inject holes into CDs, then, the CDs⁎ and Mn(II)⁎ acted as luminophors to yield CL; in the r-CDs-KMnO4 system, r-CDs were oxidized by KMnO4 directly, and CDs⁎ and Mn(II)⁎ were produced, at the same time, CL occurred. What is more interesting is that the CL intensity of the r-CD system is stronger than that of the CD system, which confirms that functional groups have strong effect on the CL behavior. It inspired us that new carbon nanoparticles with excellent luminous performance can be designed by tuning their surface groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) have been widely investigated for their unique optical properties and their potential applications in sensing [1,2], catalysis [2,3], imaging [4], and optoelectronic devices [5]. However, most conventional QDs contain heavy metals, such as Cd, which is potentially toxic and hazardous to environment and their applications are limited [6–8]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) emerged as a new kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial [9], have received much attention due to their high photostability against photo-bleaching, no blinking, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness as compared with the metal-based QDs [10–14]. Therefore, they have been successfully used in bioimaging [10,11], catalysis [12–14], and sensing [15,16]. However, the range of CDs practical applications is also scarce and further work is still necessary.

Particularly, the application of CDs as luminophor still remains sparse. The chemiluminescence (CL) properties of CDs have gained more attention recently [17–20]. For example, Lin et al. and Xue et al. [17–19] reported the CL of CDs in the presence of ONOOH or classical oxidants, and they found that the CDs could enhance the CL of H2O2–HSO3 −. Jiang et al. [20] demonstrated that the CDs could greatly enhance NaIO4–H2O2 CL reaction. In these systems, the CDs were passivated by poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG) [17–19] or doped with nitrogen atoms [20] and the CDs acted as reductant [17], luminophors [18], or energy acceptors [19,20]. However, the CL of bare CDs without passivation or doping and the effect of surface groups on the CDs CL properties were rarely investigated. Detailed investigation of these surface properties of CDs is still an interesting challenge inspiring intensive research effort.

We have used a simple reduction pathway to change the surface groups of bare CDs and found that the surface groups of CDs played an important role in their photoluminescence behavior [21]. The reduction pathway can prepare the reduced state CDs (r-CDs) without any passivating or doping treatment. Herein, we chose the bare CDs and r-CDs as models to investigate the surface group effect on the CL behaviors of CDs for the first time. CL signals can be observed after mixing these two kinds of CDs with KMnO4. The mechanism of the CL was investigated by CL spectra, fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and electron paramagnanetic resonance (EPR) spectra. Our results demonstrated that the CL behavior of the two systems is different and the CL intensity of r-CD system is much stronger than that of the CD system. We believe our research will be valuable both for understanding the relationship of CL behavior and the surface groups of CDs, and for design and synthesis of the CDs with excellent CL properties.

Lamp black was purchased from Anhui Jixi Hukaiwen Ink Industry Co. (Jixi, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), nitric acid (HNO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were from Chuandong Chemical Reagent Company (Chongqing, China). l-histidine was obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off=3500) were purchased from Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water was prepared by a Milli-Q-Plus system (USA) and used throughout.

The CDs were prepared as Liu et al. [22] and Mao et al. [23] had reported. Briefly, lamp black (0.5g) was added to HNO3 solution (5mol/L, 150mL) and the mixture was heated under reflux at 140°C for 12h. The dark-brown CD solution was neutralized with sodium carbonate. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 15min and it was then loaded into dialysis bags for dialysis against ultra-pure water for 48h. The purified CD solution was stored at 4°C for further use. To prepare r-CDs, excessive NaBH4 was mixed with the aqueous solution of CDs and then stirred gently overnight at room temperature. Excess reductant was removed by dialysis mentioned above, and the obtained r-CD solution was stored at 4°C.

CD and r-CD samples were prepared by dropping the CD and r-CD aqueous solution onto 400-mesh carbon coated copper grids (SPI, USA) and dried in a vacuum oven, respectively. The size and dispersivity were determined by HRTEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) operating at 200kV. The size distribution was determined by measuring 100 particles.

A diagram of the flow injection CL detection system was showed in Fig. 1. For CL measurement, the flow lines were inserted into the KMnO4, water, and CDs or r-CD solution, respectively. Then the pumps were started until a stable baseline was recorded. Injection was made by using an eight-way injection valve. The CL signals were recorded by an IFFL-D flow injection CL Analyzer (Ruimai Electronic Equipment Company Ltd., Xi'an, China).

The CL spectra of these systems were measured by using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the xenon lamp turned off. Fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and electron paramagnanetic resonance (EPR) spectra were measured on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and an ESP-300 E spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured using a XSAM-800 X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (KRATOS, UK).

HRTEM images ( Fig. 2, Fig. S1) show that the CDs and r-CDs are near spherical and well dispersed. The average diameters of the CDs and r-CDs were about 3.1±0.6 and 3.4±0.8nm, respectively (Fig. 2C, D). The UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra of the CDs and r-CDs were also recorded. No appreciable absorption peak is observed for the CDs (Fig. S2A). However, a new absorption peak at 280nm for the r-CDs appears in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S2B). The CDs present yellow emission while the r-CDs present blue emission under the ultraviolet radiation (360nm) (Fig. S2).

The maximum fluorescence emission of the CDs is located at 520nm with an excitation wavelength of 470nm. However, the r-CDs exhibit maximum excitation and emission wavelength near 280 and 440nm, respectively ( Fig. 3). The pH effects on the fluorescence of CDs and r-CDs were investigated. And we find that the fluorescence intensity from both CDs and r-CDs are higher at neutral solutions and lower at acidic or basic solutions (Fig. S3) [22]. The CDs were rich in oxygen-containing surface groups (carbonyl, carboxyl and epoxy) [21]. NaBH4 could selectively reduce the carbonyl and epoxy moieties to hydroxyl groups and significantly enhance the fluorescence of CDs. XPS analysis (Fig. S4) also shows that the carbonyl peak at 288.4eV decreased in intensity after reduction. The quantum yields of CDs (λ ex=470nm) and r-CDs (λ ex=280nm) are 0.59% and 3.1%, obtained by using fluorescein and quinine sulfate as the reference, respectively (Fig. S5) [21].

Acidic media have been reported to be very important for the KMnO4 induced CL systems [24,25]. Thus, H2SO4, HCl, HNO3 and H3PO4 in 1mol/L concentration were tested as the acidic medium for the r-CDs-KMnO4 system. The strongest CL signal is observed in H2SO4 medium (Fig. S6A). Concentrations of H2SO4 were optimized in a range from 0.01 to 5.0mol/L and l/1.0moL H2SO4 was chosen for further work as the Fig. S6B showed. Concentrations of KMnO4 were optimized in a range from 5.0×10−7 to 1.0×10−3 mol/L. CL intensity enhances with the increasing of KMnO4 concentration, and the CL intensity levels off as KMnO4 concentration reached 1.0×10−4 mol/L (Fig. S6C). However, higher concentration of KMnO4 cause a CL intensity decrease because the CL signal can be severely absorbed by high concentration of KMnO4 [26].

Meanwhile, the CL intensity was dependent on the concentration of the r-CDs in a certain range (Fig. S6D). The reaction conditions of the CDs-KMnO4 system were optimized (Fig. S7) and they are the same to that of the r-CDs-KMnO4 system. For comparison, the same concentration of CDs and r-CDs were selected for further experiments in the CDs system and the r-CDs system.

The kinetic study was performed to provide more details that might lead to an explanation of the reaction mechanism. After injecting 1mL of 1.0×10−4 mol/L KMnO4 into 1mL of 0.76mg/mL CDs or r-CDs in the 1.0mol/L H2SO4 solution, the kinetic curves of the CDs-KMnO4 system and the r-CDs-KMnO4 system were recorded ( Fig. 4). It shows that the CL intensity of the r-CDs system is much higher than that of the CDs system, which may be due to different groups on the surface of these two kinds of CDs. As demonstrated in our early report, there were hydroxyl groups on the surface of r-CDs [21]. And two CL peaks are found in both CL systems. The first peak with a strong CL intensity reached the maximum value in 0.2s, then quenched quickly. Meanwhile, the second peak with a weak CL intensity quenched slowly [27]. It indicated that there may be two luminous intermediates involved in the CDs-KMnO4 system and the r-CDs-KMnO4 system.

The purple color of KMnO4 solution bleaches slightly after addition of the CDs or r-CDs as showed in Fig. S8, and the characteristic absorption peak of KMnO4 near 525nm decreases [18]. More interesting, addition of the r-CDs could cause a more obvious decrease in absorbance than that of CDs. As showed in Fig. 5, after adding the CDs or r-CDs into KMnO4, the absorbance at 525nm changed from 0.473 to 0.25 and 0.217. This result can be due to the fact that the r-CDs possessed hydroxyl groups and could consume more amount of KMnO4.

To explore the CL mechanism, fluorescence spectroscopy (showed in Fig. S9A, B) was used to characterize the CDs and r-CDs before and after the CL reaction. It is found that there is no significantly change after the reaction of CDs with KMnO4 while a new emission peak around 520nm appears after the reaction of r-CDs with KMnO4. The new emission peak of r-CDs fluorescence spectra is located at the maximum emission wavelength of CDs (around 520nm), which indicates that KMnO4 oxidized hydroxyl groups on the r-CDs to carbonyl or carboxyl and increased the amount of carbonyl or carboxyl groups on the r-CDs [28]. In this process, r-CDs partly converted to CDs.

In order to further verify the interesting CL phenomena, CL spectra of the CDs-KMnO4 system and the r-CDs-KMnO4 system were collected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer with the xenon lamp turned off. Two main emission bands are observed in the CDs-KMnO4 system ( Fig. 6A). The first band locates at around 500nm, which originated from the light emission of the excited-state CDs (CDs⁎) produced by the oxidant-injected holes into the CDs [29,30]. It should be worthy of noting that the CL spectra exhibits a little blue-shift compared with the maximum fluorescence emission of the CDs. Other emission band locates at 640nm, which matches with the phosphorescence of Mn(II) (from 4T1 to 6A1 transition) [31,32]. The phosphorescence lifetime of excited-state Mn(II) (Mn(II)⁎) was long and the emission light quenched slowly [27], which accord with the second peak in the CL kinetic curves. Two CL emission could be also observed in the CL spectra of the r-CDs-KMnO4 system. A broad CL emission band located at around 500nm resembles that in the CDs-KMnO4 system. This CL emission is similar to the fluorescence spectra of CDs but different from the fluorescence spectra of r-CDs (Fig. 6B). The r-CDs could be oxidized to original CDs [21], and thus, it was reasonable to ascribe the CL emission band near 500nm to CDs⁎, which was formed by KMnO4 oxidation back to the ground state. Another weak emission band located around 640nm also matches with the phosphorescence of Mn(II).

It is well known that MnO4 − is readily reduced to Mn(II) in acid media [33]. In order to identify the oxidation state of manganese and further ascertain the emission species, EPR spectrum were used to confirm the existence of Mn(II) and the ground state properties in the CDs. As showed in Fig. 7A, CDs showed EPR signal at g=2.0033, which demonstrates a singly occupied orbital in ground state CDs [34]. The singly occupied orbital indicated that the CDs could be the electron donor and acceptor during the reaction [34,35]. After reaction with CDs and r-CDs, Six hyperfine resonances arising from high spin Mn(II) species [36] were observed in Fig. 7B, C. There are not obvious EPR signal for the KMnO4 in H2SO4 solution without the CDs or r-CDs (Fig. S10). The result indicated that KMnO4 could inject holes into the CDs or oxidize the r-CDs and was reduced to produce Mn(II)⁎ in the two systems. The Mn(II)⁎ excited state could account partially for the CL emission.

Histidine, a well known singlet oxygen quencher [37], exerted no inhibition on CL of the two CL systems (Figs. S11 and S12), which indicated that singlet oxygen emitter had no effect on the CDs system and the r-CDs system. And the CL intensity of the two systems is almost the same in oxygen, nitrogen or air saturated solutions (Figs. S11 and S12). The above results indicated that the dissolved oxygen had no effect on the CL and the singlet oxygen emitter could be excluded in both systems. And these results match with the CL behavior of the excited state Mn(II)⁎, which confirmed that the excited state Mn(II)⁎ was one emitter of the CL emission. Simultaneously, it is found that fluorescence intensity of the CDs and r-CDs could not be inhibited by histidine (Fig. S13) or dissolved oxygen (Fig. S14), and hence, it was reasonable to deduce that the excited state CDs⁎ was one of the luminophors, which relaxed energy to generate light.

Based on the above studies, the CL mechanism of the CDs-KMnO4 and r-CDs-KMnO4 systems could be summarized as shown in Fig. 8. For the CDs-KMnO4 system, KMnO4 could inject holes into the CDs and interact with the electrons of CDs to produce high energy state electrons [29,30], which made the CDs be excited. The singly occupied orbital detected by EPR spectra could serve as the hole traps [18] and the injection processes leaded to the excited state CDs⁎. While the CDs⁎ excited state returned to the ground-state accompanied with photon irradiation, the CL emission near 500nm occurred. Simultaneously, the excited state Mn(II)⁎ also produced, which was unstable and relaxed energy to generate light. For the r-CDs-KMnO4 system, r-CDs with a large amount of hydroxyl groups could react with KMnO4 easily and the excited state CDs⁎ was formed from the reaction, which returned to the ground-state with the CL emission around 500nm. The CL signal produced by the excited state CDs⁎ quenched quickly. At the same time, the r-CDs reduced KMnO4 to produce the excited state Mn(II)⁎, which backed to the ground state with CL around 610–640nm [31] and quenched slowly [27]. For the r-CDs with hydroxyl groups, the reaction of r-CDs and KMnO4 was easier than that of CDs. Therefore, The CL intensity of the r-CDs-KMnO4 system was much higher.

The CL behavior and mechanism of CDs or r-CDs in the presence of acidic KMnO4 have been studied by UV–vis, CL, fluorescence and EPR spectra. This is the first time for CDs and r-CDs as models to investigate the surface group effect on the CL behaviors of CDs. For the r-CDs with a large amount of hydroxyl groups, the CL intensity of the r-CDs-KMnO4 system is much stronger than that of the CDs-KMnO4 system in the same conditions, which indicated that the surface groups of the CDs play an important role on their CL behavior. The investigation on the CL properties of the CDs system and the r-CDs system not only gains a better understanding of the relationship of CL behavior and surface groups but also brings a broader prospect for synthesis of CDs with eximious optical properties (good CL property and high PL intensity). Furthermore, the CL of CDs opens up new opportunities for their potential applications in the analysis technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Scientific Program-Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (No. 2011CB933600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175110), the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (CSTC, 2010BB4004), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK2013A022).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2013.09.036.
